content:
Originally published at imrantech.hashnode.dev

I Thought I Was Ready. I Wasn't.

When I got my first job as a .NET developer, I genuinely believed college had prepared me well.

I knew C#. I understood databases. I had built a few small projects. I was ready.

Two weeks into the job, I realized I knew almost nothing about real software development.

This is an honest account of 2 years building production systems — what broke, what I fixed, and what I wish someone had told me before I started.

The First Few Months Were Humbling

My first real task was writing SQL queries for an HRMS system. Attendance reports, employee data, payroll calculations — all pulling from multiple tables.

I wrote the worst SQL of my life.

Bad JOINs everywhere. Stored procedures that nobody could read. API endpoints that made three times more database calls than needed. I didn't know what an N+1 problem was. I didn't know what query optimization meant in practice.

I knew SQL from college — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE. But writing SQL that actually performs well in production with real data? That was a completely different skill that nobody taught me.

My senior reviewed my code and rewrote half of it. That was embarrassing. But it was also the most educational week of my entire career.

The lesson? Knowing the syntax is not the same as knowing how to write good code.

College Projects vs Real Projects — Nobody Tells You This

In college, we built projects to submit assignments. There was a beginning, a deadline, and it ended there.

In a real job, the project never ends. You're working on a system that:

Real people use every single day

Has existing code you didn't write and must understand

Has bugs in production that affect actual employees getting paid

The biggest shock for me was seeing the overall flow of a real system for the first time.

In college, I built isolated features. In my job, I had to understand how the attendance module connects to payroll, how payroll connects to leave management, how leave management affects the HRMS reports — everything talks to everything else.

Nobody prepares you for that complexity. You only understand it by working through it.

The Bug That Made Me Lose Sleep

Six months in, I was given a task to integrate biometric attendance hardware with our HRMS system.

I had never done hardware integration before. The device communicated through a specific protocol. Data had to be pulled automatically and synced into our SQL Server database through Apache Tomcat.

Nothing worked for days.

The device was online. The server was running. But no attendance data was coming through. I checked the code a hundred times. Everything looked correct.

The problem turned out to be a network port forwarding issue — nothing to do with my code at all. Once the network was configured correctly, everything worked immediately.

That experience taught me something important: in production, the bug is not always in your code. Sometimes it's infrastructure. Sometimes it's network. Sometimes it's configuration. A good developer checks everything, not just their own code.

The Moment I Realized I Had to Get Serious

Around the one year mark, I looked at the code I had written in my first few months and felt genuinely embarrassed.

Bad variable names. No error handling. Inefficient LINQ queries. API endpoints that returned way more data than the frontend needed.

I knew I was producing working code — but not good code.

That's when I made a decision: I want to be technically strong. I want to write optimized code that I'm proud of.

I started reading about EF Core performance. I learned what query optimization actually means. I started thinking about every database call I was making and asking myself — is this really necessary?

That mindset shift changed everything about how I write code today.

What I Wish Someone Had Told Me Before Day 1 Four honest things I would tell my younger self:

Learn confidently — don't be scared of what you don't know In the beginning I was afraid to ask questions because I thought it made me look stupid. It didn't. Asking good questions is a sign of a good developer. Pretending to understand when you don't just wastes everyone's time including yours.

Improve your logical thinking deliberately - Syntax you can always look up. Logic is what separates a good developer from an average one. Practice breaking problems down before touching the keyboard. The best developers think before they type.

Use AI tools — but don't become dependent on them ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, Claude — these tools are genuinely useful and using them is not cheating. But use them to learn, not to avoid learning. When AI gives you code, understand why it works. If you can't explain it, you don't own it.

Real experience beats certifications - Nobody at my company asked about my college grades. They asked what I had built, what bugs I had fixed, and how I had improved performance. Real production experience is the most valuable thing you can build early in your career.

Where I Am Now

Two years in, I've shipped three production systems:

An HRMS platform with biometric attendance integration

An Inventory Management System

An E-Commerce application with Tally ERP integration

I've fixed N+1 problems that took API response time from 1 minute to 15 seconds. I've implemented JWT authentication with refresh token rotation. I've debugged production issues that had nothing to do with code.

Am I where I want to be technically? Not yet. That's the honest answer.

But I know exactly what I need to improve, I know how to learn, and I know that every bug I fix makes me better than I was yesterday.

One Last Thing

If you're just starting your .NET career or feel stuck in your first job — it's supposed to feel hard. The complexity is real. The learning curve is steep.

But every senior developer you admire was once staring at bad SQL wondering why nothing worked.

Keep going. Write the bad code first. Then make it better.

That's how everyone learns. 🚀

I write 3 practical .NET articles every week — real bugs, real fixes, real production lessons. Follow along if that sounds useful!
